1: Silence in heaven for about half an hour — the heavenly silence before the trumpet judgments echoes several OT texts: Hab 2:20 ("the LORD is in his holy temple; let all the earth keep silence before him"), Zeph 1:7 ("Be silent before the Lord GOD! For the day of the LORD is near"), Zech 2:13 ("Be silent, all flesh, before the LORD"). In Second Temple liturgical practice, the priests maintained silence in the temple courts while the incense offering was being made (the Tamid tractate; Luke 1:10 — the people were praying outside while Zechariah offered incense). The silence connects the seventh seal to the incense offering of vv. 3–4: the prayers of the saints, offered like incense, are the transition point from seal to trumpet.
2: The seven angels who stand before God echo the angelology of the Second Temple period: seven archangels appear in Tob 12:15 (Raphael: "I am one of the seven angels who stand ready and enter before the glory of the Lord"), and 1 Enoch 20:1–7 names Uriel, Raphael, Raguel, Michael, Saraqael, Gabriel, and Remiel as the seven holy angels. The Dead Sea Scrolls (particularly the War Scroll, 1QM) elaborate the angelic hierarchy extensively. The seven trumpets given to them recall the seven priestly trumpets (shofar) at the siege of Jericho (Josh 6:4–6), where the trumpets themselves were instruments of divine judgment rather than merely signal devices.
3: The golden censer and the incense offering connect to the Tamid sacrifice — the twice-daily offering in the Jerusalem temple that included the burning of incense on the golden altar (Exod 30:7–8; Luke 1:9–10). In the heavenly temple, the same structure applies. "A large amount of incense to offer with the prayers of all the saints" makes explicit the connection that the incense prayers in Jewish liturgy symbolized (Ps 141:2: "May my prayer be counted as incense before you"). The golden altar before the throne is the heavenly original of which the Jerusalem incense altar was the earthly copy (Exod 25:9, 40; Heb 8:5).
4: "The smoke of the incense, together with the prayers of the saints, rose up before God" — the ascending smoke as the vehicle by which prayers reach God is a liturgical commonplace in both Jewish and Greco-Roman religious practice, but in the Jewish context it is rooted specifically in the Sinai covenant liturgy: the offering of incense before the ark of the covenant (the divine dwelling place) was the closest approach to the divine presence available to any human except the high priest on Yom Kippur. The prayers of the saints ascending with the incense smoke means human prayer is not merely words spoken into the air but an act of approach to the divine presence, mediated by the liturgical framework.
5: The angel taking fire from the altar and hurling it to the earth echoes Ezek 10:2 (the man clothed in linen fills his hands with burning coals from between the cherubim and scatters them over the city) and Ezek 10:7 (one cherub gives the fire to the man). The judgment flowing from the incense altar — from the prayers of the saints — connects the suffering of the faithful community to the triggering of divine judgment: God acts in response to the prayers of those who cry "How long?" (6:10). The thunder, rumblings, lightning, and earthquake are the standard theophanic accompaniments (Exod 19:16–19; Ps 77:18; Isa 29:6).
6: The seven angels with the seven trumpets prepared to sound them — the preparation indicates a deliberate, ordered sequence of judgment. The trumpet (salpinx) in Jewish tradition served multiple functions: announcing battles (Num 10:9), calling assemblies (Num 10:3–4), heralding festivals (Lev 25:9 — the Jubilee proclaimed by trumpet on Yom Kippur), and signaling the divine presence (Exod 19:13, 16, 19; Heb 12:19). The seven trumpets combine all these functions: they announce battle (the judgment), call the assembly (of the heavenly court), and herald the presence of the holy God coming in judgment.
7: The first trumpet plague — hail and fire mixed with blood hurled onto the earth — echoes the seventh Egyptian plague (Exod 9:23–26: "The LORD rained hail on the land of Egypt; there was hail and fire flashing continually in the midst of the hail"). The addition of blood to the hail-and-fire motif combines the first plague (water to blood, Exod 7:20–21) with the seventh, creating a composite judgment image. A third of the earth, trees, and grass burned — the one-third limitation is significant: unlike the final judgment, the trumpet plagues are partial, allowing space for repentance (the text notes, at 9:20–21, that those not killed still did not repent).
8: The second trumpet — "something like a great mountain blazing with fire" thrown into the sea — may echo the volcanic eruption of Vesuvius in 79 CE, which the seven churches of Asia Minor would have known about (the eruption killed tens of thousands and sent ash across the eastern Mediterranean). The image of a blazing mountain thrown into the sea may also draw on Jer 51:25 ("I am against you, O destroying mountain, declares the LORD... I will stretch out my hand against you and roll you down from the crags and make you a burnt mountain") — a judgment oracle against Babylon applied now to the sea. A third of the sea became blood echoes Exod 7:20–21.
9: The destruction of a third of sea creatures and a third of ships echoes the pattern of limited judgment in the Exodus plagues (the plagues affected portions of Egypt while sparing Israel) but on a cosmic scale. Ships destroyed — Rome's Mediterranean trading network, the economic lifeline of the empire, is attacked. The empire that made the sea its highway of commerce (the mare nostrum — "our sea") finds its economic infrastructure destroyed. This prepares the later commercial lament of ch. 18.
10: The third trumpet — a star blazing like a torch falling from heaven onto rivers and springs — evokes the Exodus plague of bitter water (Exod 15:23–25, the waters of Marah) expanded to a cosmic scale. Falling stars in Jewish apocalyptic signify fallen angels or divine agents (Isa 14:12 — the fall of the "morning star"; 1 Enoch 86:1–3 where fallen stars corrupt the earth's waters). The star-as-divine-agent falling to contaminate the fresh water supply represents a judgment on the interior of the land, as the sea judgment (v. 8) represented a judgment on the maritime periphery.
11: The name of the star, Wormwood (Apsinthos), connects to the prophetic tradition of bitter-water judgment: Jer 9:15 ("I will feed this people with wormwood and give them poisonous water to drink") and Jer 23:15 (the same formula for false prophets). Wormwood is a bitter plant (Artemisia absinthium) used medically in antiquity; its bitterness was proverbial for suffering and judgment. Many people dying from the bitter waters echoes the Exodus plague (Exod 7:21: "the fish in the river died, and the river smelled so bad that the Egyptians could not drink its water"), but the scale is now rivers and springs rather than one river.
12: The fourth trumpet — striking of sun, moon, and stars by a third — echoes the ninth Egyptian plague (Exod 10:21–23: "dense darkness... for three days"). The Day-of-the-LORD cosmic darkness traditions (Isa 13:10; Amos 8:9; Joel 2:10) underlie the imagery. The one-third reduction (a third of each luminary struck) differs from the total darkness of the Exodus plague and the complete cosmic disruption of the sixth seal (6:12–14), reinforcing the partial-judgment character of the trumpets. Day shortened, night shortened — the disruption of the regular rhythms of creation signals the destabilization of the natural order under divine judgment.
13: The eagle (aetos) flying in midheaven and crying "Woe, woe, woe!" introduces the final three trumpets as a distinct and more severe series — the "three woes" (8:13; 9:12; 11:14). The eagle as a bird of ill omen echoes Deut 28:49 ("the LORD will bring a nation against you from far away, from the end of the earth, swooping down like an eagle") — the eagle of Rome's legions is now the eagle that announces judgment on Rome's world. Ouai ouai ouai (woe woe woe) is the prophetic lament formula (Isa 3:11; 5:8–23; Amos 5:18) adapted into an angel's announcement; the triple form signals three distinct woes, each more severe than the previous trumpets.
